propagation_methods: Stem cuttings, water propagation, soil propagation
cutting_type: Stem node cutting (with at least one node and aerial root)
rooting_hormone_required: No (roots easily without it, but hormone can speed things up)
rooting_hormone_type: Optional — IBA (Indole-3-butyric acid) based rooting hormone
air_layering_possible: Yes(aerial roots at itswrap a node in moist moss/soil)
division_possible: Yes (if multiple stems/clumps present at soil level)
seed_propagation_possible: No (rarely flowers indoors; not practically propagated from seed)

cutting_possible: Yes — a node with at least one aerial root can be cut and will grow into a new plant
propagation_methods: Stem cuttings, water propagation, soil propagation, air layering
cutting_type:
Stem node cutting (must include at least one node,
 Ideally with an aerial root attached)
rooting_hormone_required:
No — roots without it, though success is faster/more 
Reliable 
With it
rooting_success_rate:
High (70–90%, depending on whether the 
Cutting has an aerial root)
water_propagation_possible:
Yes — nodes will root directly 
in water
air_layering_possible:
Yes — wrapping a node (especially one with an aerial root) in moist 
moss while still attached to the mother plant will induce rooting
 at that point
division_possible:
Yes — if the plant has multiple stems/crowns growing
 From the base, these can be separated with roots intact
seed_propagation_possible:
Yes — technically works if viable seed is obtained (requires the 
plant to flower and fruit, which is rare indoors, but the technique
 Itself is possible)
grafting_possible:
No — Monstera doesn't form compatible vascular 
graft unions the way woody trees do; this technique
 Doesn't work on it

cutting_possible: Yes — a stem cutting with at least one node and leaf can be cut and will grow into a new plant
propagation_methods: Stem cuttings, air layering, soil propagation
cutting_type: Stem tip cutting or single node cutting with leaf
rooting_hormone_required:
No — but strongly recommended as rubber plant cuttings can 
Be slow to root without it
water_propagation_possible:
Yes — cuttings can root in 
water, though soil or moss 
rooting tends to give more 
Reliable results
air_layering_possible: Yes — one of the most reliable methods for rubber plant; wrapping a notched stem section in moist moss induces rooting at that point, and is especially useful for tall leggy plants
division_possible:
No — rubber plant grows as a single-stemmed tree and does 
not produce multiple crowns or offshoots at the base that can be 
Divided
seed_propagation_possible:
Yes — technically possible if viable seed is obtained, but 
rubber plant rarely flowers outside its native tropical habitat, 
Making seed extremely difficult to source
grafting_possible:
No — not a standard or practically viable technique 
For Ficus elastica

cutting_possible: Yes — stem cuttings with at least one node can be cut and will grow into a new plant
propagation_methods:
Stem cuttings, cane cuttings, water propagation, soil propagation,
 Division
cutting_type:
Stem tip cutting or cane section cutting (a bare section of 
stem with at least one node, even without leaves, can root 
And sprout)
rooting_hormone_required: No — roots reasonably well without it, but hormone improves success rate
water_propagation_possible: Yes — stem tip cuttings root readily in water
air_layering_possible:
Yes — wrapping a node on the main cane in moist moss will 
Induce rooting at that point, useful for tall bare-stemmed plants
division_possible:
Yes — if the plant has produced multiple offshoots or 
basal shoots at soil level, these can be separated with 
Roots intact and potted individually
seed_propagation_possible:
Yes — technically possible from seed if viable seed is obtained,
 but Dieffenbachia rarely flowers indoors and seed is difficult 
To source commercially
grafting_possible: No — not a standard or practically viable technique for Dieffenbachia

cutting_possible:
Yes — stem cuttings with at least one node and leaf can be cut and will grow into a new 
Plant
propagation_methods: Stem cuttings, water propagation, soil propagation, division
cutting_type:
Stem tip cutting or stem section cutting with at least one 
Node and leaf
rooting_hormone_required: No — roots well without it, though hormone can improve success rate
water_propagation_possible: Yes — stem cuttings root readily in water
air_layering_possible:
Yes — wrapping a node on the main stem in moist moss will 
Induce rooting at that point, particularly useful for tall leggy specimens
division_possible:
Yes — one of the most common and reliable methods; mature
 Aglaonema naturally produces multiple stems and offshoots at the
 Base that can be separated with roots intact and potted individually
seed_propagation_possible:
Yes — technically possible from seed if viable seed is 
obtained from the plant's berries, but rarely done as it is 
Slow and division/cuttings are far more practical
grafting_possible: No — not a standard or practically viable technique for Aglaonema

cutting_possible: Yes — stem cuttings with at least one node can be cut and will grow into a new plant
propagation_methods: Stem cuttings, water propagation, soil propagation, division
cutting_type: Stem tip cutting or stem node cutting with at least one node and leaf
rooting_hormone_required: No — roots very easily without it, though hormone can slightly improve speed
water_propagation_possible: Yes — nodes root very readily in water
air_layering_possible: Yes — wrapping a node in moist moss while still attached to the mother plant will induce rooting at that point
division_possible:
Yes — mature Syngonium naturally produces multiple stems and offshoots at 
The base that can be separated with roots intact and potted individually
seed_propagation_possible:
Yes — technically possible from seed if viable seed is obtained,
 But Syngonium rarely flowers indoors making seed very difficult to source
grafting_possible: No — not a standard or practically viable technique for Syngonium

cutting_possible: Yes — stem cuttings with at least one node can be cut and will grow into a new plant
propagation_methods: Stem cuttings, soil propagation, air layering, grafting, division, seed propagation
cutting_type: Stem tip cutting or hardwood/softwood stem cutting with at least 2–3 nodes
rooting_hormone_required: Yes — strongly recommended as rose cuttings root much more reliably with rooting hormone
water_propagation_possible: Yes — rose cuttings can root in water though soil rooting gives more reliable and stronger root development
air_layering_possible: Yes — bending a cane down and burying a wounded node section in moist soil or wrapping in moist moss will induce rooting at that point
division_possible: Yes — own-rooted roses (not grafted ones) can be divided at the root clump if multiple crowns are present
seed_propagation_possible: Yes — roses produce seeds inside rosehips; seeds can be germinated but offspring will not be identical to the parent plant as most garden roses are hybrids
grafting_possible: Yes — one of the few plants on your list where grafting is a standard and widely practiced commercial propagation method; rose cultivars are commonly budded or grafted onto hardy rootstocks for stronger plants

cutting_possible: Yes — stem cuttings with at least one node can be cut and will grow into a new plant
propagation_methods: Stem cuttings, soil propagation, air layering, seed propagation
cutting_type: Stem tip cutting or softwood/semi-hardwood stem cutting with at least 2–3 nodes
rooting_hormone_required: No — but strongly recommended as it significantly improves rooting success and speed
water_propagation_possible: Yes — cuttings can root in water though soil rooting tends to produce stronger root systems
air_layering_possible: Yes — wrapping a wounded stem section in moist moss while still attached to the mother plant will induce rooting at that point
division_possible: No — Hibiscus rosa-sinensis grows as a single-stemmed shrub and does not produce dividable multiple crowns or offshoots at the base
seed_propagation_possible: Yes — Hibiscus produces seeds from its seed pods after flowering; seeds germinate readily but offspring of hybrid cultivars will not be identical to the parent plant
grafting_possible: Yes — grafting is practiced commercially to combine desirable flowering cultivars onto stronger rootstocks, though it is not commonly done by home gardeners

cutting_possible: Yes — stem cuttings with at least one node can be cut and will grow into a new plant
propagation_methods: Stem cuttings, soil propagation, air layering, division
cutting_type: Stem tip cutting or semi-hardwood stem cutting with at least 2–3 nodes
rooting_hormone_required: No — but strongly recommended as it significantly improves rooting success and speed
water_propagation_possible: Yes — cuttings can root in water though success rate is lower than soil rooting for Mogra
air_layering_possible: Yes — wrapping a wounded stem section in moist moss while still attached to the mother plant will induce rooting at that point; works reliably on Mogra
division_possible: Yes — mature Mogra plants naturally produce multiple stems and basal shoots that can be separated with roots intact and potted individually
seed_propagation_possible: Yes — Mogra produces seeds technically, but most commonly grown cultivars like Grand Duke of Tuscany are double-flowered and sterile producing no viable seed, so seed propagation is practically not possible for most garden Mogra
grafting_possible: No — not a standard or practically viable technique for Jasminum sambac

cutting_possible: Yes — stem cuttings with at least one node and leaf can be cut and will grow into a new plant
propagation_methods: Stem cuttings, soil propagation, air layering, seed propagation
cutting_type: Stem tip cutting or stem section cutting with at least 2–3 nodes and leaves
rooting_hormone_required: No — but strongly recommended as Croton cuttings can be slow and reluctant to root without it
water_propagation_possible: Yes — cuttings can root in water though success rate is lower than soil rooting; milky sap should be allowed to dry on the cut end for a few hours before placing in water
air_layering_possible: Yes â€” one of the more reliable methods for Croton; wrapping a wounded stem section in moist moss induces rooting at that point and avoids the transplant shock that Croton cuttings are prone to
division_possible: No — Croton grows as a single-stemmed shrub and does not produce dividable multiple crowns or offshoots at the base
seed_propagation_possible: Yes — Croton produces seeds that can be germinated, but offspring of named cultivars will not be identical to the parent plant as color and leaf pattern vary significantly from seed
grafting_possible: No — not a standard or practically viable technique for Codiaeum variegatum

cutting_possible: Yes — stem cuttings with at least one node can be cut and will grow into a new plant
propagation_methods: Stem cuttings, soil propagation, seed propagation, root suckers
cutting_type: Stem tip cutting or semi-hardwood stem cutting with at least 2–3 nodes
rooting_hormone_required: Yes — strongly recommended as Curry Leaf cuttings are notoriously difficult to root without hormone assistance
water_propagation_possible: No — Curry Leaf cuttings do not root reliably in water; soil or rooting medium is required
air_layering_possible: Yes â€” wrapping a wounded stem section in moist moss while still attached to the mother plant will induce rooting at that point and is actually more reliable than cuttings for this plant
division_possible: Yes — mature Curry Leaf plants naturally produce root suckers and basal shoots around the base that can be carefully separated with roots intact and potted individually; this is one of the most reliable propagation methods for this plant
seed_propagation_possible: Yes — fresh seeds from ripe black berries germinate well; seed is actually one of the most reliable propagation methods for Curry Leaf but seeds must be fresh as they lose viability very quickly after harvest
grafting_possible: No — not a standard or practically viable technique for Murraya koenigii

cutting_possible: Yes — stem cuttings with at least one node can be cut and will grow into a new plant
propagation_methods: Stem cuttings, water propagation, soil propagation, seed propagation
cutting_type: Stem tip cutting with at least 2–3 nodes and leaves
rooting_hormone_required: No — Tulsi roots very easily without any rooting hormone
water_propagation_possible: Yes — Tulsi stem cuttings root very readily in water within 7–14 days
air_layering_possible: Yes — technically possible as the stems will root at nodes when in contact with moist medium, but rarely practiced as cuttings and seeds are far simpler and faster
division_possible: Yes — mature bushy Tulsi plants can be carefully divided at the root clump if multiple stems with roots are present, though seed and cuttings are more commonly used
seed_propagation_possible: Yes — one of the most common and reliable propagation methods for Tulsi; seeds germinate very quickly and easily, and the plant self-seeds readily if flower spikes are left on the plant
grafting_possible: No — not a standard or practically viable technique for Ocimum tenuiflorum

cutting_possible: Yes — leaf cuttings can be taken but success rate is low; stem cuttings with a crown are more reliable
propagation_methods: Offsets/pups, division, stem crown cutting, seed propagation
cutting_type: Crown stem cutting (top rosette with a short stem section) or leaf cutting (low success rate)
rooting_hormone_required: No — offsets and crown cuttings root without hormone, though it can improve leaf cutting success
rooting_success_rate:
Very High for offsets/pups (90–95%); 
Low for leaf cuttings (20–40%)
propagation_difficulty:
Very Easy via offsets/pups; 
Difficult via leaf cuttings
water_propagation_possible: No — Aloe cuttings and offsets do not root reliably in water; soil or well-draining rooting medium is required
air_layering_possible: No — Aloe Vera is a rosette-forming succulent without elongated climbable stems making air layering physically not applicable to this plant
division_possible: Yes — one of the most reliable and common methods; mature Aloe naturally produces offsets (pups) around the base that can be separated with roots intact and potted individually
seed_propagation_possible: Yes — Aloe Vera produces seeds from its flower spikes; seeds germinate reasonably well but plants are very slow growing from seed and take years to reach a useful size
grafting_possible: No — not a standard or practically viable technique for Aloe vera
